title: Massage for rehabilitation after total knee arthroplasty: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.

items.ai_reason: Title states: “a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.”
Background section explains KOA, TKA, massage use, gaps in prior evidence, and need for a systematic review/meta-analysis.
Objectives are clearly stated in Abstract and end of Background, aiming to evaluate massage effectiveness for postoperative TKA rehabilitation.
Selection criteria section details PICOS-based inclusion and explicit exclusion criteria and main outcome domain (postoperative rehabilitation indices).
Search strategy section lists all databases and states searches from inception to May 2024, giving last search date range.
Search strategy mentions key terms and states full strategies are in Supplementary Table S1.
Data extraction section describes screening process, two independent reviewers, title/abstract then full-text screening, and third reviewer for disagreements.
Data extraction section states two researchers independently extracted specified data items and resolved disagreements via discussion/third reviewer.
Outcomes (postoperative pain, knee ROM, D-dimer, length of stay, adverse events, other functional scores) are listed and defined via named scales and time points.
Paper lists non-outcome data items (age, sex, etc.) but does not define all such variables in detail or describe assumptions about missing/unclear information.
Quality assessment section specifies Cochrane Risk of Bias Tool, domains assessed, and does so for each included study; risk-of-bias assessors implied as study authors.
Statistical analysis section specifies MD or SMD with 95% CI for continuous outcomes; all reported syntheses use these measures.
Eligibility for each synthesis beyond overall inclusion criteria is not explicitly described (e.g. how specific time points or measures were chosen per synthesis).
No description of handling missing summary statistics, imputations, or data conversions beyond choice of MD/SMD is provided.
Use of tables for study characteristics and forest plots for each outcome is described and referenced in figures and Table 1.
Statistical analysis describes fixed vs random-effects models, heterogeneity assessment (Q, I²), software (RevMan, Stata), and meta-analysis rationale.
Subgroup analysis by country type to explore heterogeneity in length of stay is described in Methods and applied in Results.
Methods and Results explicitly state that no sensitivity analysis was performed and provide the reason (small number of studies per outcome).
Publication bias assessment using Begg’s and Egger’s tests is described, with outcomes and p-values reported; this addresses risk of bias from missing results.
Although evidence is informally described as low-to-moderate quality, no formal certainty assessment method (e.g. GRADE) or procedures are described.
Study selection section reports numbers identified, excluded at each step, reasons for exclusions, and mentions a flowchart figure.
Reasons for excluding 10 full-text articles (e.g., not RCT, wrong intervention, data not integrable) are provided in the Study selection section.
Table 1 cites each included study and presents country, age, sample size, sex, interventions, duration, time points, and main outcomes.
Risk of bias section summarizes domain-level assessments and refers to a detailed figure presenting risk of bias for each included study.
Results provide pooled MDs and some sample sizes, but do not systematically present per-study group summary statistics and individual effect estimates for all outcomes.
Characteristics of included studies are summarized but, for each synthesis, risk-of-bias distribution among contributing studies is not explicitly summarized alongside the synthesis.
For each meta-analysis, pooled MDs with 95% CIs, p-values, and I² are reported, with direction of effect described.
Subgroup analysis for length of hospital stay presents separate pooled estimates for China vs other countries and interprets heterogeneity reduction.
No sensitivity analyses were conducted; only the absence of such analyses is reported, so no results can be presented.
Publication bias assessments via Begg’s/Egger’s are reported for relevant syntheses, including identification of possible bias for day-14 pain outcome.
Although authors refer to ‘low- to moderate-quality evidence,’ they do not present structured certainty assessments by outcome using a defined method or summary table.
Discussion interprets findings on pain, ROM, D-dimer and length of stay in light of prior evidence on mechanisms, DVT prevention, and rehabilitation practices.
Limitations of the evidence (publication bias, geographic concentration in China, difficulty with blinding, small numbers, heterogeneity) are discussed explicitly.
Discussion notes review-process limitations including possible missed gray literature and absence of sensitivity analyses affecting robustness.
Implications for practice (massage as adjunct therapy) and future research (multicenter, better methods, standardized massage, more outcomes, adverse event grading) are discussed.
Methods state PROSPERO registration and provide registration number CRD42023411680.
They state that the protocol was registered but do not indicate where the full protocol can be accessed or whether a detailed protocol was prepared.
No amendments to the registered protocol are described or explained in the text.
Funding section declares ‘Not applicable’ and elsewhere no sponsors or funders are described, implying no external financial or non-financial support.
Competing interests section states that the authors have no competing interests.
They state that datasets are available from the corresponding author on request but do not report any materials as publicly available or provide repositories/links.
Title explicitly states this is “a systematic review and meta-analysis.”
Abstract clearly states the aim is to evaluate effectiveness of massage for postoperative rehabilitation after TKA.
Only inclusion of randomized controlled trials on massage after TKA is mentioned; no explicit exclusion criteria are described.
Abstract lists databases (PubMed, Web of Science, EMBASE, Cochrane Library, CNKI) and search period (from inception to May 2024).
Abstract states the Cochrane Risk of Bias Assessment Tool was used to assess risk of bias.
Abstract specifies that a meta-analysis of specified outcomes was performed, indicating synthesis method.
Abstract reports 11 randomized controlled trials with 940 subjects and notes key characteristics (massage vs control in TKA rehab).
Abstract reports main outcomes, direction of effect for pain, ROM, D-dimer, adverse events, and hospital stay, with number of included studies and participants overall.
Abstract notes that study heterogeneity was large, indicating a limitation of the evidence.
Abstract concludes massage can be used as an adjunctive treatment and summarizes implications for pain, ROM, and D-dimer.
Abstract does not mention any funding source for the review.
Abstract does not provide a register name or registration number.

title: Parameters of Spinal Cord Stimulation in Complex Regional Pain Syndrome: Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials.

items.ai_reason: Title includes the phrase “Systematic Review and Meta-analysis,” clearly identifying the report as a systematic review.
Introduction explains CRPS, existing SCS use, gaps in knowledge about SCS parameters, and why a review of RCTs is needed.
Objectives are explicitly listed as three aims near the end of the introduction.
Methods state inclusion and exclusion criteria (RCTs, CRPS patients, SCS parameters) and that RCTs of various SCS modalities were grouped for meta-analysis on LF-SCS vs conventional/placebo.
Databases are named (PubMed, Embase, CINHLA) but no specific last search dates are reported.
Only keywords are mentioned; full search strategies, including filters and limits for each database, are not presented.
Selection process (number of reviewers, independence, and any automation tools) is not described.
Data collection methods, including number of reviewers, independence, and any contact with investigators, are not reported.
Pain relief is identified as the primary outcome, but not all outcomes are fully listed and definitions/time points for all outcomes sought are not clearly detailed.
Other data items (e.g., participant characteristics, funding of included studies) and assumptions about missing or unclear information are not described.
Risk of bias methods are described: RoB 2 tool used, five domains plus crossover S domain, two independent reviewers, disagreement resolution process.
Effect measures are specified: weighted mean difference (MD) for pain scores and GPE with 95% confidence intervals.
The process for deciding which studies contributed to each synthesis is only implicitly described; no explicit method beyond outcome availability is reported.
No methods for data preparation (e.g., handling missing statistics, conversions) are described.
Although figures and tables are referenced, the methods used to tabulate or visually display results are not described in the methods section.
Use of DerSimonian and Laird random-effects meta-analysis, MD with 95% CI, heterogeneity (I²), and RevMan 5.4 software is reported with rationale of heterogeneity.
No methods to explore causes of heterogeneity (e.g., subgroup analysis, meta-regression) are described.
Sensitivity analysis method is described: stepwise exclusion of individual trials to assess changes in pooled estimates.
No methods are reported for assessing risk of bias due to missing results or reporting bias.
No methods for assessing certainty or confidence in the body of evidence (e.g., GRADE) are described.
Results section reports numbers identified, screened, excluded, and included; a PRISMA flow diagram is referenced (Fig. 1).
Excluded studies that appeared potentially eligible are not listed or individually cited with reasons for exclusion.
Each included trial is cited and its design, interventions, and SCS parameters are summarized, including in Table 1.
Risk of bias assessments for each included study are presented, with judgments (low, some concerns, high risk) summarized.
Individual study-level summary statistics and effect estimates with precision for all outcomes are not fully presented in structured tables or plots in the provided text.
Syntheses results are presented, but there is no explicit summary of risk of bias and key characteristics per synthesis beyond brief comments.
Meta-analysis results for pain and GPE are reported with MDs, 95% CIs, P values, and I²; direction of effects is described.
No investigations of heterogeneity causes (e.g., subgroup or meta-regression analyses) are reported.
Sensitivity analysis results are presented in text and Table 2, showing pooled estimates with each study omitted.
No results are presented regarding risk of bias due to missing results or reporting biases.
No certainty or confidence assessments (e.g., GRADE ratings) for any outcome are reported.
Discussion interprets findings in light of prior literature on SCS, CRPS incidence, costs, and previous reviews.
Limitations of included evidence are discussed, including small number of RCTs, mixed neuropathic populations, and limited follow-up.
Limitations of the review process are discussed, such as search strategy focusing on RCTs and challenges in data extraction/analysis.
Implications for practice and future research on SCS parameters, patient preference, and need for longer follow-up are discussed.
No registration information or explicit statement that the review was not registered is provided.
No information about a review protocol or statement that no protocol was prepared is provided.
No amendments to registration or protocol are described.
Disclaimer states there was no external funding in preparation of the manuscript, implying absence of funder role.
Conflict-of-interest statement declares that authors have no commercial associations posing a conflict.
Availability of data, extracted datasets, analytic code, or other materials is not reported.
The title explicitly states it is a “Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials.”
The abstract states the objective: “To provide evidence for the use of SCS to treat CRPS and characterize the additional benefits of various SCS waveforms.”
It notes inclusion of RCTs comparing SCS parameters for CRPS but provides no explicit inclusion or exclusion criteria details.
Databases (PubMed, Embase, CINHLA) are listed, but no search dates or last search date are reported in the abstract.
It describes risk-of-bias assessment, stating how many studies were low, some concerns, or high risk of bias.
It specifies that a meta-analysis was conducted and mentions pooled and pooled analyses of RCT designs, indicating synthesis methods.
It reports the number of included RCTs (four) but does not give the total number of participants or summarize study characteristics.
It presents main outcome results with number of studies, summary estimates, 95% CIs, P values, and indicates LF-SCS superiority over comparators.
It briefly notes limitations: mixed neuropathic conditions, pooled different RCT designs, and one high-risk-of-bias study.
It provides an interpretation and implications: LF-SCS is superior to conventional therapy/placebo, but more evidence is needed for novel parameters.
No information on the primary source of funding is provided in the abstract.
The abstract does not report any register name or registration number.

title: Is the combination of exercise therapy and health education more effective than usual medical care in the prevention of non-specific back pain? A systematic review with meta-analysis.

items.ai_reason: The article is explicitly labelled “SYSTEMATIC REVIEW” in the title section.
Introduction describes burden, existing exercise/education evidence, prior reviews, and the gap regarding combined interventions, providing clear rationale.
Primary and secondary objectives are explicitly stated near the end of the Introduction.
Inclusion and exclusion criteria are detailed under Study selection; review focuses on RCTs with ET+HE versus usual care in NBP.
All databases searched and the month/year of search (December 2021) are specified in Data sources and searches.
Full search strategies for each database, including terms and combinations, are presented in Table 1.
Study selection section specifies two reviewers, independent screening, handling of disagreements, and use of full texts; no automation tools mentioned.
Data extraction section specifies two independent reviewers, a customized extraction table, and conflict resolution by discussion.
Primary and secondary outcomes are named, but there is no explicit listing/definition of all outcomes nor clarification about all measures/time points sought.
Data items (demographics, sample characteristics, intervention details, outcomes, instruments, follow-up) are listed; no assumptions about missing data are described, but none are claimed.
Use of Jadad and PEDro scales is described, including two independent reviewers performing quality assessment.
Effect measure is specified as standardized mean difference (SMD) with 95% confidence intervals.
Eligibility for synthesis beyond overall inclusion criteria is not explicitly described (e.g. per outcome, per comparison) other than stating when possible results were gathered.
No explicit description of data preparation methods such as handling missing statistics or conversions is provided.
It notes that tables and forest plots were used, but does not describe the specific methods for tabulating or visually displaying results.
Meta-analysis methods, SMDs, heterogeneity via I2 and Q-test, and CMA V2 software are described; a rationale for combining studies is implicit via similar interventions/comparators.
No methods to explore causes of heterogeneity (e.g. subgroup or meta-regression) are described.
No sensitivity analysis methods are described.
No methods for assessing reporting bias or missing results (e.g. funnel plots, statistical tests) are reported.
No approach to assess certainty or confidence in the body of evidence (e.g. GRADE) is reported.
Study selection results (numbers at each stage) are described, and a PRISMA flow diagram is referenced.
Excluded full-text studies that appeared eligible are not cited nor are reasons for their exclusion detailed.
Characteristics of each included study (sample, interventions, inclusion/exclusion) are provided in Tables 3 and 4, citing each trial.
Risk of bias/quality assessments for each study using PEDro and Jadad scores are presented in Tables 2 and 3.
Individual study summary statistics and effect estimates with precision are not fully reported for all outcomes; only selected between-group differences are described.
Results section does not for each synthesis briefly summarise both characteristics and risk of bias of contributing studies together.
Meta-analysis results for pain and disability include SMDs, 95% CIs, p-values, and I2, with direction of effects reported.
No investigations of heterogeneity causes (e.g. subgroups, meta-regression) are reported, so no such results are presented.
No sensitivity analyses were conducted or reported.
No assessment or presentation of risk of bias due to missing results or reporting biases is provided.
No certainty/confidence ratings for any outcome are reported.
Discussion interprets findings relative to prior reviews and guidelines on exercise and education for NBP.
Limitations section discusses limitations of the included evidence, such as heterogeneity and lack of subgroup analyses.
Limitations of the review process (e.g. lack of subgroup analyses, restricted populations) are noted in the Limitations section.
Implications for clinical practice and need for further research and specific protocols are discussed in Discussion and Conclusions.
PROSPERO registration information and ID (CRD42022311026) are clearly provided.
Location or accessibility of the full review protocol is not stated, nor is its absence explicitly mentioned.
No amendments to registered information or protocol changes are described.
Funding section states there is no funding and implies no sponsor role.
Disclosure statement explicitly reports no potential conflicts of interest.
Data availability statement notes datasets are available from the corresponding author but does not specify publicly available materials or repositories.
The title explicitly states it is “A systematic review with meta-analysis.”
The abstract clearly states the aim: to determine whether exercise plus education is more effective than usual medical care in preventing non-specific back pain.

Inclusion criteria are explicitly reported (years, intervention components, population). No explicit exclusion criteria are given, but PRISMA requires inclusion and/or exclusion; inclusion is specified.
Databases (PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, Medline) are listed, but no search dates or last-search date are reported.
Risk of bias assessment is implied by reporting the PEDro score (average 6.5), a standardized methodological quality/risk-of-bias tool for trials.
The abstract states that a meta-analysis was conducted and reports standardized mean differences with 95% confidence intervals and p-values, indicating quantitative synthesis methods.
The abstract reports the total number of included studies (4 RCTs) but not the total number of participants or a summary of key study characteristics.
Main outcomes (pain intensity and disability) are reported with SMDs, 95% CIs, p-values, and direction of effect favoring exercise plus education.
No limitations of the included evidence (e.g., risk of bias, inconsistency, imprecision, sample size) are mentioned in the abstract.
The abstract provides an interpretation: interventions combining exercise and education seem more effective than usual care, with implications for prevention of non-specific back pain.
The abstract does not mention any funding source for the review.
No register name or registration number is provided in the abstract.

title: Effect of Repetitive Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation on Post-stroke Dysphagia: A Meta-analysis of Stimulation Frequency, Stimulation Site, and Timing of Outcome Measurement.

items.ai_reason: Title text provided does not clearly identify the article as a systematic review or meta-analysis.
Introduction describes dysphagia after stroke, existing treatments, prior meta-analyses, and gaps in knowledge, establishing the rationale for the review.
Objectives are explicitly stated: to evaluate effects of rTMS on post-stroke dysphagia by stimulation frequency, site, and timing of outcomes.
Inclusion and exclusion criteria are specified, including population, intervention, comparison, study design, language, and data requirements for meta-analysis.
All databases searched (PubMed, SCOPUS, Embase, Web of Science, Cochrane Library) and the date range up to 13 December 2021 are reported.
Only keywords are listed; the full search strategies for each database, with exact strings and limits, are said to be in Section 1 but not provided in the text given.
Screening and selection are described: duplicates removed; two independent reviewers screened titles/abstracts and full texts; disagreements resolved by consensus.
Data extraction methods are described, including use of two independent reviewers, handling of graphical data, and contacting authors for missing information.
Outcomes (e.g., PAS, SSA) are mentioned, but there is no explicit list/definition of all outcomes sought nor clarification about inclusion of all compatible results.
Other data items (e.g., demographics, rTMS protocol) are mentioned, but assumptions about missing/unclear information are not described.
Risk of bias methods are specified: Cochrane Handbook tool domains, categorization (low/high/unclear), and resolution of discrepancies by consensus.
Effect measures are specified: standardized mean differences in change from baseline with 95% confidence intervals for continuous outcomes.
Eligibility for each synthesis (by outcome/time-point) is implicit but no explicit description of processes used to decide study inclusion per synthesis is provided.
Data preparation methods are described, including converting PAS ordinal scores to parametric data and calculating correlations when unknown from other articles.
Text refers to figures and a table but does not describe the methods used to tabulate or visually display individual study and synthesis results.
Synthesis methods are described: fixed-effects model, use of RevMan 5.3, Cochran’s Q for heterogeneity, SMD calculation, and significance threshold.
No methods are reported for exploring heterogeneity sources such as subgroup analyses beyond pre-planned parameter stratification, or meta-regression.
No sensitivity analysis methods are described in the Methods section.
There is no description of any methods to assess risk of bias due to missing results or reporting biases (e.g., funnel plots, statistical tests).
No methods are reported for assessing certainty or confidence in the body of evidence (e.g., GRADE).
Results section reports numbers of records identified, duplicates removed, records excluded, and six included studies, with reference to a flow diagram.
Some excluded RCTs that seemed eligible are cited in the Discussion with explicit reasons for exclusion (e.g., missing SDs, different outcomes, protocol differences).
Each included study is cited and key characteristics (design, sample size, age, interventions, outcomes, follow-up) are presented in Table 1 and text.
Risk-of-bias assessments for each study across Cochrane domains are summarized and presented in a figure (Fig. 2).
Summary statistics and effect estimates are reported only at synthesis level; per-study group means, SDs, and effect estimates for each outcome are not presented.
For each synthesis, the text summarizes contributing studies’ characteristics (frequency/site, timing, outcomes) and mentions overall risk-of-bias patterns.
Meta-analysis results for each synthesis are presented with SMDs, 95% CIs, p-values, and comments on direction of effects; heterogeneity via Cochran’s Q is reported.
No results of investigations into causes of heterogeneity (e.g., subgroup/meta-regression beyond predefined groupings) are presented.
No results of sensitivity analyses are reported.
No assessments of risk of bias due to missing results (reporting bias) for any synthesis are presented.
No certainty or confidence assessments (e.g., GRADE ratings) for any outcome are reported.
Discussion interprets findings versus previous meta-analyses and individual studies, explaining consistencies and differences in effect sizes and protocols.
Limitations of the included evidence are discussed, including small number of studies, heterogeneity of outcomes, and exclusion of some RCTs.
Limitations of review processes are discussed, e.g., exclusion of studies without PAS/SSA, protocol restrictions, and potential underestimation due to active controls.
Implications for clinical use of rTMS, need for standardized outcomes, optimal protocols, and future RCTs are discussed.
No registration information (e.g., PROSPERO number) is reported, nor is it stated that the review was not registered.
There is no mention of a review protocol or where it can be accessed, nor a statement that no protocol was prepared.
No amendments to registration or protocol are described.
Funding sources and their roles are described: study had no external funding; support from a Korean NRF grant is noted.
Authors explicitly declare that they have no conflict of interest.
Availability of data, code, or other materials (e.g., extraction forms, datasets, analytic code) is not reported.
Title identifies the report as a meta-analysis but does not explicitly state it is a systematic review.
Abstract clearly states the objective: to evaluate effects of rTMS parameters (frequency, site, timing) on post-stroke dysphagia.
Only randomized controlled trials on rTMS effects are mentioned; no explicit inclusion and exclusion criteria are specified.
Abstract lists databases (PubMed, SCOPUS, Embase, Cochrane Library) and search date range (Jan 1, 1980–Dec 13, 2021).
No methods for assessing risk of bias in the included studies are described.
Use of standardized mean difference, confidence intervals, and P values indicates meta-analytic synthesis methods.
Abstract reports six included studies, total participants (158), and group allocation (81 rTMS, 77 sham).
Main outcomes (SSA, PAS) are reported with SMDs, 95% CIs, P values, and direction of effect by group and time point.
Abstract does not provide any statement on limitations of the included evidence, such as risk of bias or imprecision.
Concluding sentence interprets that certain rTMS protocols improve swallowing function after stroke and specifies timing.
No information is provided about the primary source of funding for the review.
No register name or registration number is reported in the abstract.

title: Do Patients with Prostate Cancer Benefit from Exercise Interventions? A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.

items.ai_reason: Title states: “A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.”
Introduction reviews burden of prostate cancer, treatment effects, prior reviews, and remaining gaps to justify the review.
Objectives are explicitly stated at the end of the introduction, including examining differential effects by exercise modality.
Eligibility criteria (study design, participants, interventions, comparators, and duration) are clearly specified in Section 2.2.
All databases and other sources are listed, with search month/year and trial registry access date reported.
Supplementary Material File S1 is stated to contain the complete MEDLINE search strategy.
Section 2.4 describes multi-step screening, two independent reviewers, and conflict resolution by a third author.
Section 2.5 states who extracted data, that a second reviewer checked independently, and how multiple reports were handled.
Section 2.5 lists and defines all primary outcomes for which data were sought; use of end-of-intervention time points is described in 2.6.
Paper lists other variables (e.g., participant characteristics in Table 1) but does not explicitly define all non-outcome data items or describe assumptions for missing/unclear data.
Section 2.7 specifies use of Cochrane risk of bias tool, domains excluded, and that assessments were performed, with summaries in supplementary files.
Section 2.8 specifies use of weighted mean difference or standardized mean difference with 95% confidence intervals for continuous outcomes.
Eligibility for each synthesis is implied but no explicit description of processes used to decide which studies contributed to each outcome synthesis is provided.
Section 2.8 explains handling of missing SDs, use of baseline SD or SE-derived SD, and handling of multi-arm trials.
Use of forest plots and summary tables/figures is described via references to figures and supplementary files presenting individual and pooled results.
Section 2.9 details random-effects meta-analysis model, heterogeneity statistics (chi-squared, I²) and software (STATA), with rationale noted.
Section 2.11 describes prespecified subgroup analyses by exercise modality and assessment instrument to explore heterogeneity.
Section 2.11 lists several sensitivity analyses (change scores, model type, excluding clusters, pilots, outliers) to test robustness.
Section 2.10 describes assessment of publication bias using funnel plots and Egger’s test when >10 studies are available.
No methods are reported for using GRADE or any other formal approach to assess certainty/confidence in the body of evidence.
Results section 3.1 reports numbers of records identified and included; a PRISMA-style flow diagram is referenced as Figure 1.
Studies that were excluded after full-text review are not individually cited with reasons for exclusion.
Section 3.2 and Table 1 cite all included trials and describe key characteristics (sample, treatment, intervention, control, outcomes).
Section 3.3 states that risk-of-bias summaries and detailed reasons are presented in Supplementary Material Files S2 and S3.
Paper presents pooled effects; it does not systematically report, for each study and outcome, both group summary statistics and individual effect estimates with precision.
Synthesis results sections summarise characteristics and mention risk-of-bias assessments globally, with references to supplementary RoB tables for contributing trials.
For meta-analysed outcomes, pooled estimates, CIs, and heterogeneity measures are reported, with direction of effect described in text and figures.
Results section describes findings from subgroup analyses exploring modality and instrument as sources of heterogeneity, with corresponding forest plots in supplements.
Results mention sensitivity analyses (e.g., exclusion of outliers, change scores) and their impact, with supporting forest plots in supplementary files.
Section 2.10 and results for QoL and cardiovascular fitness report funnel plots and Egger’s tests as assessments of reporting bias for those syntheses.
No results are presented from any formal certainty or confidence assessment (e.g., GRADE) for the outcomes.
Discussion section 4.1 interprets findings in light of previous reviews and related evidence, comparing magnitudes and consistencies.
Section 4.2 discusses limitations of included evidence, such as heterogeneity, underrepresentation of certain patient groups, and adherence/fidelity reporting issues.
Section 4.2 notes limitations of the review process, including inability to perform some subgroup analyses and variability in intervention content and reporting.
Discussion and Conclusions outline implications for clinical exercise prescription and suggest directions for future research on modalities and target populations.
Section 2.1 reports registration in PROSPERO with register name and number CRD42020153851.
Section 2.1 notes that a protocol was registered in PROSPERO, implying where it can be accessed.
No amendments to the registered protocol or explanations of changes are described.
Funding statement explicitly notes no external funding; role of funders is therefore inherently absent.
Conflicts of interest section states that authors declare no conflict of interest.
Data Availability Statement only invites readers to contact the corresponding author; no materials are stated to be publicly available or where to find them.
The title explicitly states this is “A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.”
The abstract states the aim: to review the effect of exercise on QoL and metabolic health in men with prostate cancer.
The abstract mentions “eligible trials” but does not specify any inclusion or exclusion criteria.
It notes a systematic search in nine databases but does not name the databases or provide search dates.
There is no description of any methods used to assess risk of bias of included trials.
It states that eligible trials were included in a meta-analytic procedure and reports SMDs and CIs, indicating quantitative synthesis methods.
It reports 33 randomized controlled trials with 2567 participants, i.e., total number and basic study characteristics.
Main outcomes are presented with SMDs and 95% CIs, including direction and modality comparisons for several outcomes.
The abstract does not summarize limitations of the included evidence, such as risk of bias, inconsistency, or imprecision.
It provides an overall interpretation, concluding exercise improves metabolic health, aerobic exercise is superior, and QoL effects are small.
No information on the primary source of funding is reported in the abstract.
No register name or registration number is provided in the abstract.

title: Effects of High-Intensity Strength Training in Adults With Knee Osteoarthritis: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials.

items.ai_reason: Title includes the phrase “A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis,” clearly identifying it as a systematic review.
Introduction summarizes existing evidence, conflicting previous findings, and the need to clarify effects of high‑intensity strength training in knee osteoarthritis.
Objective is explicitly stated: to assess effects of high‑intensity strength training versus low‑intensity or routine care on pain, function, quality of life, and adverse events.
Inclusion/exclusion criteria are explicitly listed, including population, intervention, comparator, outcomes, design, language, and exclusions by type and data completeness.
All databases (PubMed, Embase, Cochrane Library, Web of Science) and the last search date (up to March 10, 2021) are clearly reported.
Search terms are reported and a full PubMed strategy is said to be in Supplementary Table 1; filters/limits (English) are described in eligibility criteria.
Selection process states only that two authors searched and then screened; it does not specify how many screened each record/report or whether screening was independent.
Data extraction was done by two authors independently, with items listed and disagreements resolved by discussion; no automation tools are mentioned.
Outcomes (pain, function, quality of life, adverse events) and all specific measurement instruments used for each are clearly listed and defined.
Other data items (demographics, design, interventions, follow‑up, Jadad score) are listed; no assumptions about missing/unclear information are described, so none are claimed.
Risk of bias assessment methods, tool (RevMan domains), and that two authors independently evaluated with discussion of disagreements are clearly reported.
Weighted mean differences for continuous outcomes and relative risks for dichotomous outcomes, both with 95% CIs, are explicitly specified.
Eligibility for the overall review is given, but no process is described for deciding which included studies contributed to each specific synthesis beyond outcome availability.
No methods are reported for handling missing summary statistics, imputation, or data conversions prior to synthesis.
Use of forest plots for each outcome and reference to structured supplementary tables summarizing study results are explicitly described.
Meta‑analysis methods are detailed: fixed vs random‑effects based on I²/Q, effect measures, heterogeneity assessment, and use of STATA 15.1 are reported.
The authors explicitly state that the source of heterogeneity between studies was not analyzed; no subgroup or meta‑regression methods are described.
Sensitivity analyses by omitting one study at a time are described in Methods and reported in Results as supporting robustness.
No specific methods are described for assessing risk of bias due to missing results or reporting bias (e.g., funnel plots, small‑study effects).
No method such as GRADE or other formal certainty/confidence assessment is reported for the body of evidence.
Results describe numbers identified, screened, full texts assessed, and 10 included studies, and mention a PRISMA flow diagram (Fig. 1).
Although 33 full‑text articles were reviewed, no list or citations of excluded studies with reasons is reported.
The text states that characteristics of all 10 included studies are in Supplementary Table 2 and cites each included trial in the text.
Risk‑of‑bias assessments for each study are presented via Figures 2A and 2B, summarizing judgments for all domains.
For all meta‑analyzed outcomes, pooled data with WMDs and CIs are given, and figures/tables (forest plots, Supplementary Table 3) provide study‑level summary statistics and effect estimates.
Syntheses results are given, and overall risk‑of‑bias patterns are described, but characteristics and risk of bias are not summarized per synthesis.
All statistical syntheses report summary estimates, 95% CIs, P values, and I², with direction of effect described for group comparisons.
No investigations of causes of heterogeneity (e.g., subgroup or meta‑regression results) are presented.
Results section reports that leave‑one‑out sensitivity analyses showed the overall evaluation did not depend on any single study.
No results or judgments are presented regarding risk of bias from missing results or reporting biases for any synthesis.
No certainty or confidence ratings (e.g., GRADE) for any outcome are reported in the Results.
Discussion interprets findings, compares with previous reviews and related exercise studies, and places results in the context of broader evidence.
Limitations of the included evidence are discussed, including self‑reported outcomes, partial reporting of adverse events, and gender/English‑only constraints.
Authors acknowledge review‑level limitations such as not analyzing heterogeneity sources, language restriction, and possible exclusion due to insufficient intervention descriptions.
Implications for clinical practice and future research, including defining intensity criteria and improving reporting and safety assessment, are explicitly discussed.
No registration information (e.g., PROSPERO) is provided, and it is not stated that the review was unregistered.
There is no mention of a review protocol or where it can be accessed, nor a statement that no protocol was prepared.
No amendments to a registration or protocol are described.
Financial disclosure is provided, stating that no conflicts and no funder role affecting content are reported; this describes support context.
Statement explicitly notes that no conflicts of interest have been reported by the authors or individuals controlling the article content.
Although supplementary materials are noted, there is no explicit statement about public availability of data, code, extraction forms, or analytic datasets.
The title explicitly states this is a “Systematic Review and Meta-analysis.”
The abstract clearly states the aim: to assess effects of high-intensity versus low-intensity strength training or routine care in adults with knee osteoarthritis.
The abstract does not describe any inclusion or exclusion criteria for studies beyond population and interventions.
It specifies PubMed, Embase, Cochrane Library, Web of Science and the last search date (up to March 10, 2021).
No methods for assessing risk of bias in included studies are mentioned in the abstract.
The abstract does not describe how results were synthesized or presented (e.g., meta-analysis methods, fixed/random-effects).
States that 10 studies including 892 subjects were included and they all had knee osteoarthritis.
Reports meta-analytic result with pooled WMD, 95% CI, P-value for peak torque, and notes non-significant differences for multiple outcomes between groups.
No limitations of the evidence (e.g., risk of bias, inconsistency, imprecision) are mentioned in the abstract.
Concludes that high-intensity training has similar effects and comparable safety, interpreting the overall findings and implications for practice.
The abstract does not report any information on funding sources for the review.
No register name or registration number is provided in the abstract.

title: Meta-Analysis of the Effect of Yoga Practice on Physical Fitness in the Elderly.

items.ai_reason: Title identifies it as a meta-analysis but does not explicitly identify it as a systematic review.
Introduction clearly explains the need to synthesize evidence on yoga’s effects on elderly physical fitness in context of existing studies.
Objective is explicitly stated in abstract and introduction as meta-analyzing effects of yoga on physical fitness in the elderly.
Eligibility criteria are specified: age ≥60, single yoga program, physical fitness outcomes, pre–post experimental design, reported statistics for effect size.
All databases (Cochrane, Embase, PubMed) are named and the exact search date (25 March 2021) is reported.
Only key terms are mentioned; the full search strategies, including all terms, filters, and limits for each database, are not presented.
Selection process states two investigators independently selected studies and resolved disagreements by discussion; PRISMA flow diagram is referenced.
Methods for data extraction (who extracted, independence, procedures to obtain/confirm data) are not described.
Outcome domains are listed only as broad physical fitness categories; outcomes are not fully defined, nor is selection of time points/measures described.
Variables beyond basic participant and intervention characteristics are minimally listed, and no assumptions about missing or unclear information are described.
No methods or tools for assessing risk of bias in included studies are reported.
Effect size is specified as Cohen’s d for result parameters; this is the effect measure used in syntheses and tables.
No description of how studies were allocated to particular syntheses beyond broad subgrouping by fitness factor, age, and period.
No description of data preparation methods such as handling missing statistics or data conversions is provided.
Tables and funnel plot are presented but methods for tabulating or visually displaying results are not described in the methods section.
Synthesis methods describe use of fixed or random-effects models based on Q and I², and CMA 3.0 software; heterogeneity criteria are given.
Subgroup analyses by age and intervention period to explore heterogeneity are described in methods and results.
No sensitivity analyses (e.g., excluding studies, alternative assumptions) are described.
Publication bias assessment using funnel plot, Egger’s regression, and trim-and-fill is described.
No method for assessing certainty or confidence in the body of evidence (e.g., GRADE) is reported.
Numbers of records identified, included, and exclusion process are summarized, and a PRISMA 2020 flow diagram is referenced.
Excluded studies that appeared eligible are not individually cited with reasons for exclusion.
All included studies are cited and their key characteristics (design, participants, interventions, outcomes) are presented in Table 1.
No risk-of-bias assessments for individual studies are presented.
Effect sizes and CIs are presented only at aggregated/subgroup levels; per-study summary statistics and effect estimates for each outcome are not shown.
Syntheses results are reported, but characteristics and risk of bias of contributing studies are not summarized together for each synthesis.
Meta-analytic summary estimates, CIs, heterogeneity statistics, and direction of effects are presented for all syntheses (overall and subgroups).
Results of subgroup analyses by age and intervention period, exploring heterogeneity, are presented in Table 3 and text.
No results from sensitivity analyses are reported.
Reporting bias due to missing results is assessed and presented via Egger’s test and trim-and-fill adjusted effect size.
No assessments of certainty or confidence in the evidence for outcomes are reported.
Discussion interprets the findings and compares them with prior evidence on yoga and physical fitness/health in older adults.
Limitations of the included evidence (e.g., unclear yoga type, publication bias, lack of prospective studies) are discussed.
Limitations of review processes, such as categorical moderator choices and exclusion of non-RCT publication types, are described.
Implications for practice (promoting yoga) and suggestions for future research (e.g., types of yoga, broader study types) are discussed.
Registration information is provided: PROSPERO registration number CRD42021247535.
Location or accessibility of the review protocol is not stated beyond the registration number.
No amendments to the registered protocol are described.
Funding source (2021 Research Fund of University of Ulsan) is reported; role of funder is implied as non-influential but not detailed.
Author states there is no conflict of interest.
Data Availability Statement says 'Not applicable' and does not specify any publicly available data, code, or materials.
Title calls it a meta-analysis but does not identify it explicitly as a systematic review.
Objective is clearly stated as meta-analyzing effects of yoga intervention on physical fitness in the elderly.
No inclusion or exclusion criteria are specified in the abstract.
Information sources (Cochrane, PubMed, Embase) and last search date (25 March 2021) are reported.
Methods for assessing risk of bias in included studies are not mentioned.
Abstract states that a meta-analysis was done but does not specify methods for presenting or synthesizing results.
Total number of included studies is given, but number of participants and study characteristics are not reported.
Main outcome directions are given, but no numbers of studies/participants per outcome or summary estimates/intervals are reported.
No limitations of the evidence (e.g., risk of bias, inconsistency, imprecision) are described.
Provides a general interpretation and implications, recommending yoga to improve fitness and wellbeing in the elderly.
No information on the primary source of funding is provided in the abstract.
No register name or registration number is mentioned.

title: Systematic review and meta-analysis on the effectiveness of ultrasound-guided versus landmark corticosteroid injection in the treatment of shoulder pain: an update.

items.ai_reason: Title includes the phrase “Systematic review and meta-analysis,” explicitly identifying the report as a systematic review.
Introduction explains current practice, conflicting evidence, and lack of guidance, situating the rationale in existing studies and reviews.
Objectives are explicitly stated: to investigate effectiveness and safety of US-guided versus landmark corticosteroid injections in adults with shoulder pain.
Methods specify inclusion (adult RCTs, English, US-guided vs landmark CS) and exclusion (non-RCTs, non-original reports), and that all eligible RCTs were meta-analysed together by outcomes.
All databases searched are listed and the search end date (up to July 3, 2021) is provided, plus manual reference checking.
Only keywords are listed; full search strategies are said to be in a Supplementary file, which is not reproduced in the provided text.
Two investigators screened titles/abstracts and full texts independently and in duplicate; this clearly describes the selection process and number of reviewers.
Data extraction is described as done independently using a data collection form, and the specific data items extracted are detailed.

All outcomes for which data were sought (VAS, multiple functional and disability scales, abduction, side effects, cost-effectiveness) are listed and defined, with specified time point (baseline to 6 weeks).
Other variables (age, sex, diagnosis, etc.) are listed, but no description of assumptions for missing or unclear information is provided.
They state use of Cochrane Risk of Bias Tool, list domains assessed, and note disagreements were resolved by consensus, implying at least two assessors.
Effect measures (SMD, MD, RR with 95% CI) are specified for outcomes in the Statistical analysis section.
Eligibility for synthesis is not described beyond general inclusion; no explicit process for deciding which studies contributed to each specific synthesis.
They do not describe methods for handling missing statistics, imputations, or data conversions beyond choosing SMD/MD/RR.
They report using forest plots for each outcome and present a characteristics table; these are standard tabulation/visual display methods.
They describe meta-analytic models (Mantel–Haenszel fixed-effects, comparison with random-effects in sensitivity), heterogeneity measures (I², Q), and software (R, meta package).
Subgroup analyses (e.g., VAS 0–10 vs 0–100, types of functional/disability scores) are described to explore heterogeneity.
They describe sensitivity analyses using random-effects models and leave-one-out meta-analyses to test robustness.
Publication bias is assessed with funnel plots, but no specific method is used to assess risk of bias due to missing results within syntheses.
No method such as GRADE or equivalent is reported for assessing certainty or confidence in the body of evidence.
Results section reports numbers identified, duplicates, screened, full-texts assessed, and studies included, and notes a PRISMA flow diagram.
They state that a list of excluded studies with main reasons is provided in a Supplementary file.
All included RCTs are cited, and Table 1 presents key study and patient characteristics for each.
Risk-of-bias assessment results are summarized (e.g., randomization, blinding, incomplete data) and detailed summary is said to be in Supplementary file.
Pooled effect estimates are presented, but per-study group summary statistics and individual-study effect estimates with precision are only in figures/tables not included in the text provided.
For each synthesis, they summarize included study numbers, patient totals, and comment on risk of bias and heterogeneity across contributing RCTs.
For each meta-analysis they report pooled SMD/MD/RR with 95% CI, I², p-values, and direction of effect.
They present results of subgroup analyses intended to explore heterogeneity (e.g., by scale type, VAS range) and report whether findings were consistent.
They report that leave-one-out and model-based sensitivity analyses did not materially change results, and this is described per outcome in general terms.
Although funnel plots were used to check publication bias, explicit assessments of risk of bias due to missing results for each synthesis are not presented.
No formal or informal certainty-of-evidence judgments (e.g., GRADE ratings) are presented for any outcome.
Discussion interprets main findings and compares them with several prior systematic reviews and meta-analyses.
They discuss limitations of the included evidence, including short follow-up, risk of bias, lack of blinding, and small sample sizes.
They acknowledge review-level limitations such as inability to assess long-term outcomes and heterogeneity not resolved by analyses.
They discuss clinical implications of US-guided injections and highlight need for adequately powered RCTs and cost-effectiveness research.
They do not report any registration information (e.g., PROSPERO) or state that the review was not registered.
No protocol location is provided, and they do not state that a protocol was not prepared.
There is no description of amendments to registered information or protocol, or statement that no amendments occurred.
Funding section identifies support (STDF/EKB) and states ‘None to declare’ regarding other funding; role of funder is implicitly limited to open access support.
Declarations section states “Conflict of interest None to declare,” addressing competing interests.
They only note that supplementary material is available but do not specify which materials (data, code, forms) are publicly available or where to find each.
The title explicitly states this is a “Systematic review and meta-analysis.”
Objective is clearly stated: to assess effectiveness of ultrasound-guided versus landmark corticosteroid injections in adult patients with shoulder pain.
Abstract notes adult patients and RCTs but does not specify full inclusion and exclusion criteria for the review.
Databases are listed, but the abstract does not report the date when each source (or any source) was last searched.
No methods for assessing risk of bias in included studies are described in the abstract.
Abstract specifies meta-analysis methods, including effect measures (MD, SMD, RR), confidence intervals, software, and subgroup/leave-one-out analyses.
Abstract reports 18 RCTs with 1010 patients and identifies key characteristics (comparison of US-guided vs landmark corticosteroid injections for shoulder pain).
Presents main outcome results with effect sizes, confidence intervals, and indicates direction of effect favoring ultrasound-guided injection; non-significant outcomes are also reported.
Abstract does not summarise limitations of the evidence (e.g., risk of bias, inconsistency, imprecision).
Provides a general interpretation that ultrasound-guided injections are effective and notes need for further cost-effectiveness research, indicating implications.
No information on funding source is provided in the abstract.
The abstract does not mention any registration, register name, or registration number.

title: Effectiveness of eHealth Self-management Interventions in Patients With Heart Failure: Systematic Review and Meta-analysis.

items.ai_reason: Title includes the term “systematic review,” clearly identifying the report as such.
Introduction explains HF burden, existing eHealth evidence, knowledge gaps, and need for this review in context of prior studies.
Explicit aim is stated: to systematically review and meta-analyze the effectiveness of eHealth self-management interventions in HF patients.
Inclusion and exclusion criteria are detailed using the PICO framework, including design, language, population, intervention, comparator, and outcomes.
All databases are named with search date range and last search date (July 12, 2022) reported.
Search terms are summarized but full, reproducible strategies for each database, including filters/limits, are not presented.
Study selection used Rayyan; titles/abstracts and full texts screened with reference to two reviewers, and disagreements resolved with a third reviewer.
Two independent reviewers extracted data; synthesis done independently by two researchers, with third reviewer resolving disagreements.
All primary and secondary outcomes are listed and defined (mortality, readmissions, QoL, HF knowledge, self-care, medication adherence).
Non-outcome variables extracted are listed, but no description of assumptions for missing or unclear information is provided.
RoB 2 tool is specified; domains described; assessments done independently by two reviewers with third reviewer consultation.
Effect measures are specified: Mantel-Haenszel odds ratios for dichotomous outcomes and standardized mean differences for continuous outcomes.
Eligibility for each synthesis beyond overall inclusion criteria is not described; no explicit process for mapping studies to specific syntheses.
Handling of missing summary statistics or data conversions is not described beyond noting exclusion of studies with imperfect data.
Use of forest plots and tables is implied but methods for tabulation or visual display are not explicitly described in the Methods.
Meta-analytic models, heterogeneity assessment (Q, I²), criteria for fixed vs random effects, and software (RevMan, R) are described with rationale.
Subgroup analyses by recruitment setting are planned/performed to explore heterogeneity in primary outcomes.
Sensitivity analyses using a leave-one-out approach for primary outcomes are described.
Reporting bias assessment methods are described: funnel plots and Egger tests for all primary outcomes.
GRADE is specified to assess certainty for each outcome, including domains and use of GRADEpro software.
Numbers of records identified, screened, full-text reviewed, and included are reported, and a PRISMA flow diagram is referenced.
Excluded studies that appeared potentially eligible are not individually cited with specific reasons for exclusion.
All included RCTs are cited; study characteristics are summarized in text, a main table, and Multimedia Appendix 1.
Risk of bias results for each study are presented and summarized in Figure 2 with domain-level judgments.
Pooled effect estimates are presented, but individual-study group summaries and per-study effect estimates with precision are not fully reported in text.
Synthesis results focus on pooled effects; there is no explicit summary per synthesis of contributing study characteristics alongside risk of bias.
For each meta-analyzed outcome, pooled effect sizes with CIs, P values, and heterogeneity (I², Q test) are reported, with direction of effect described.
Results of subgroup analyses by population setting for all primary outcomes are reported and summarized.
Results of leave-one-out sensitivity analyses are described, with reference to Multimedia Appendix 3 showing stability of findings.
Assessments of reporting bias using funnel plots and Egger tests are reported, stating no publication bias detected for primary outcomes.
Certainty of evidence (GRADE ratings) for each outcome is presented alongside results (e.g., low, moderate, very low quality).
Discussion interprets findings in relation to broader eHealth and HF literature and prior systematic reviews.
Limitations of included evidence are discussed, including heterogeneity, self-report bias, sample representativeness, and language restriction.
Limitations of the review process such as English-only search and heterogeneity issues are acknowledged.
Implications for practice, implementation challenges, policy issues, and priorities for future research in eHealth self-management are discussed.
Registration information is provided: PROSPERO registry with specific registration number CRD42021246973.
Location or accessibility of the full review protocol is not described; only registration is mentioned.
No amendments to the registered protocol are described or explained.
Sources of financial or non-financial support and the role of funders or sponsors are not reported.
A conflicts of interest statement is provided, explicitly declaring none.
Availability of data extraction forms, extracted data, analytic datasets, code, or other materials is not reported.
The title explicitly states this is a “Systematic Review and Meta-analysis,” identifying it as a systematic review.
The abstract clearly states the aim is to systematically review the evidence for the effectiveness of eHealth self-management in patients with heart failure.

The abstract specifies inclusion criteria: randomized controlled trials, eHealth vs usual care, adult HF patients, time frame 2011–2022; exclusion criteria are implied by not meeting these conditions.
The abstract names the databases (EMBASE, PubMed, CENTRAL, CINAHL) and provides the search period (January 1, 2011, to July 12, 2022).
The abstract states that the Cochrane Risk of Bias tool (RoB 2) was used to assess risk of bias for each study.
The abstract notes that meta-analyses were performed using RevMan and R, indicating quantitative synthesis methods.
It reports 24 RCTs with 9634 participants and describes key characteristics (adult HF patients, eHealth vs usual care).
The abstract provides effect estimates (ORs, SMDs) with 95% CIs and p-values for main outcomes, indicating direction of effect compared with usual care.
Limitations of evidence are mentioned via GRADE ratings (low/very low/moderate quality) and the statement that more high-quality studies are needed.
The abstract interprets that eHealth self-management benefits HF patients in various ways and notes implications that effects are influenced by multiple aspects.
The abstract does not report any information on the primary source of funding for the review.
The abstract does not provide a register name or registration number for the review.

title: A systematic review and meta-analysis of elastic stockings for prevention of thrombosis after orthopedic surgery.

items.ai_reason: Title is not provided in the excerpt, so it is unclear whether it identifies the report as a systematic review.
Introduction explains DVT after orthopedic surgery, existing uncertainty about elastic stockings, and rationale for doing a meta-analysis to clarify preventive effect.
Objectives are clearly stated: to investigate the preventive effect of elastic stockings on DVT after orthopedic surgery via literature search and meta-analysis.
Methods section clearly details inclusion and exclusion criteria and specifies grouping by surgical type (ankle, hip/knee arthroplasty, spinal surgery).
Databases (PubMed, Embase, Cochrane) are named, but no specific search dates or last search dates are reported.
Only keywords and limits are provided; full search strategies for each database, including filters and exact strings, are not reported.

Selection process states two researchers searched, screened abstracts, then full texts according to criteria; implies independent work, though independence is not explicit but process is described with reviewer numbers.
Data collection section does not state how many reviewers extracted data, whether they worked independently, or any procedures for resolving discrepancies or contacting investigators.
Outcomes are implicit (incidence of DVT) but the paper does not explicitly list and define all outcomes sought or explain selection of time points or measures.
Lists some extracted variables but does not define all variables, mention funding of included studies, or describe assumptions for missing/unclear information.

Risk of bias assessment uses Cochrane criteria, describes six domains, and indicates that studies were evaluated accordingly, with results in Table 2; number of assessors is not explicit but a method is specified.
Methods specify odds ratio (OR) with 95% confidence interval as the effect measure for DVT incidence using Mantel-Haenszel method.
Eligibility for each synthesis/subgroup is only briefly described (by surgery type); no detailed process for matching studies to specific syntheses is provided.
Describes converting percentages to case numbers and conducting sensitivity analysis and handling heterogeneity; this is a method to prepare data for synthesis.
States that data were organized into tables, and that forest plots and funnel plots were created to display results of studies and syntheses.
Meta-analysis methods include random vs fixed effects models, Mantel-Haenszel method, I² and Q for heterogeneity, and use of RevMan 5.3.5 software, with rationale based on heterogeneity.
Explores heterogeneity by sensitivity analysis excluding one study and by subgroup analyses based on type of surgery (ankle vs hip/knee arthroplasty).
Sensitivity analysis is explicitly described, excluding one study to test impact on heterogeneity and results.
Reporting bias is mentioned only via a funnel plot, but no explicit method to assess risk of bias due to missing results is described in Methods.
No method such as GRADE or other formal approach is described for assessing certainty or confidence in the body of evidence.
Results state initial 90 studies and inclusion of 7, reference a flowchart (Figure 1) describing search and screening, fulfilling the selection process description.
The paper does not list or cite excluded studies that appeared potentially eligible nor provide reasons for their exclusion.
Each included study is cited and key characteristics (sample size, age, sex, surgery type, interventions, DVT events) are presented in Table 1.
Table 2 provides risk of bias assessments for each study across multiple domains based on Cochrane criteria.
Table 1 reports DVT event counts but not full group summary statistics or per-study effect estimates with precision for all outcomes.
Synthesis results describe characteristics and provide some risk of bias comments, but do not explicitly summarize risk of bias among studies per synthesis/subgroup.
Presents overall and subgroup meta-analytic ORs with 95% CIs, Z and P values, and heterogeneity (I², P) for each synthesis and describes direction of effect.
Reports results of heterogeneity exploration via subgroup analyses (ankle vs hip/knee) including I² and different effect sizes between subgroups.
Presents results of sensitivity analysis excluding the spinal surgery study, with new I² and effect estimates reported.
Although a funnel plot is shown and publication bias suggested, no explicit assessment or conclusion is presented as a ‘risk of bias due to missing results’ per synthesis.
No results are reported regarding certainty or confidence in the evidence using any formal or informal framework.
Discussion interprets findings versus prior meta-analyses and individual studies, explaining differences and clinical meaning in the context of other evidence.
Limitations of included evidence are discussed, including heterogeneity, publication bias, differences in stocking types, pressure, and intervention duration.
Authors acknowledge limitations of their review methods, such as not distinguishing types of stockings and not recording duration of interventions.
Implications for clinical practice and need for combined prophylaxis and further research are explicitly discussed.
States completion of PRISMA checklist but provides no registration information and does not explicitly state that the review was unregistered.
Mentions PRISMA reporting checklist availability, but not a review protocol or its access location, nor that no protocol exists.
No amendments to registration or protocol are described.
Funding is clearly reported as ‘None,’ implying no funder role.
Conflicts of interest statement declares that authors have no conflicts of interest.
No statement is made about availability of data extraction forms, extracted data, analysis datasets, code, or other materials.
The title explicitly states this is a systematic review and meta-analysis.
The abstract clearly states the objective: to investigate the preventive effect of elastic stockings on DVT after orthopedic surgery.
Only randomized controlled trials and a publication time frame are mentioned; no explicit inclusion and exclusion criteria are described.
Databases are listed (PubMed, Embase, Cochrane) and a time frame is mentioned, but no specific last search dates are reported.
The abstract does not mention any methods used to assess risk of bias in the included studies.
It specifies that RevMan was used for statistical analysis and that meta-analysis with random/fixed effects and subgroup analyses were conducted.
The abstract reports 7 included studies with a total of 3,116 patients and notes they are randomized controlled trials after orthopedic surgery.
Main outcome results are presented with OR, 95% CI, P-values, heterogeneity, and subgroup results for the different surgery types.
The abstract does not provide any summary of limitations of the included evidence, such as risk of bias or imprecision.
It interprets that elastic stockings are effective for ankle surgery but not for other surgeries and suggests combining with anticoagulants and other therapies.
No information about the primary source of funding is provided in the abstract.
The abstract does not report any register name or registration number for the review.
